question_text: A student designs a circuit with a combination of series and parallel components: two resistors (R₁ and R₂) are connected in series with each other, and this series combination is then connected in parallel with a third resistor (R₃). How does the voltage across R₁ compare with the voltage across R₃?

options.label: A
options.text: The voltage across R₁ equals the voltage across R₃ because they are both in the same circuit

options.label: B
options.text: The voltage across R₁ is less than the voltage across R₃; the series combination receives less voltage than the parallel resistor

options.label: C
options.text: The voltage across R₁ exceeds the voltage across R₃ because the series connection increases voltage

options.label: D
options.text: The voltages are equal only if R₁ and R₃ have identical resistance values

correct_answer: The voltage across R₁ is less than the voltage across R₃; the series combination receives less voltage than the parallel resistor
explanation: In this mixed circuit, R₃ is connected in parallel with the series combination of R₁ and R₂. When components are in parallel, they experience the same voltage across them. Therefore, the voltage across the entire (R₁ + R₂) series combination equals the voltage across R₃. However, R₁ only receives part of this voltage because it must share it with R₂ in the series path. So the voltage across R₁ alone is less than the voltage across R₃.
